title: Demystifying Physics: Word Problems vs. The Real World
author: Tklesson Team
content:
Physics word problems look like they're written in another language. A block slides down a ramp. A car accelerates from rest. Two trains leave stations at the same time. It's overwhelming. But here's the secret: physics is just math with a story. Learn how to translate the story, and the math becomes easy.

Step 1: Read the Problem for WHAT, Not HOW

First, ignore the numbers. Just figure out what's happening. Is something moving? Is something falling? Is something pushing? Draw a quick picture in your head. If you can visualize the scene, you're halfway there.

Don't start writing equations yet. Just ask: What objects are involved? Are they moving? Is anything changing? The physics is in the relationships, not the arithmetic.

Step 2: List What You Know (the Givens)

Go back through the problem and pull out every number and fact. Write them down in a list with their variables. For example:
• Initial velocity = 0 m/s (the car started from rest)
• Time = 5 seconds
• Acceleration = 2 m/s²
• Unknown = final velocity

Label everything with units. Units tell you what kind of quantity you're dealing with. Meters = distance. Seconds = time. Meters per second = velocity. If you mix them up, you'll get nonsense answers.

Step 3: Identify the Physics Concept

Most physics problems test one or two core ideas. Is this about forces (Newton's laws)? Energy (kinetic vs potential)? Motion (kinematics)? Waves? Electricity? Once you know the topic, you know which equations you're allowed to use.

This is where reading the chapter before attempting the homework matters. The problem set is designed to practice the equations from that chapter. If it's the kinematics chapter, use the kinematics equations. Don't overcomplicate it.

Step 4: Write Down the Relevant Equation

Don't memorize every equation. Memorize the patterns. For constant acceleration motion (which is most intro physics), there are only four kinematic equations. Choose the one that contains the variables you have and the one you're solving for.

If you have initial velocity, time, and acceleration, and you want final velocity, use: v = v₀ + at.
If you don't have time, use: v² = v₀² + 2aΔx.

Choose the equation that fits your knowns. This is pattern matching, not magic.

Step 5: Plug in Numbers with Units

Now do the math. But keep the units in the calculation. If your answer comes out in the wrong units, you made a mistake. If you're solving for meters per second and your answer is in kilograms, something is wrong.

Write every step. Don't skip algebra. Most mistakes happen when you try to do too much in your head. Show your work, even on simple steps.

Step 6: Check if Your Answer Makes Sense

Does your answer pass the common sense test? If a car accelerates at 2 m/s² for 5 seconds, its speed increases by 10 m/s (about 22 mph). That's reasonable. If you got 500 m/s, you probably multiplied instead of added.

Also check the sign (positive/negative). If you dropped a ball from a tower, its velocity should be negative if you defined upward as positive. If your answer is positive, you lost a sign somewhere.

Physics word problems are puzzles. They follow rules. Learn the rules, translate the story, and the numbers will tell you what to do.
